data.title: CoreWeave: Power Ramp Meets Capital Reality
data.description:
Seven months ago, my focus was on the evolving story of CoreWeave (CRWV), shifting from a GPU cloud provider to a stealth mode backbone for independent AI workloads, and the test is whether it can convert the existing backlog into margins ahead of the capital cycle reset. Today, the story is quite different. The backlog is not the issue; the issue is the speed of delivery and capital management.

I do not believe that CRWV is entering the Feb. 26 print with a beat-or-miss scenario. The market is looking at the revenue consensus for the following quarter, which is $1.53 billion, a normalized estimate of a loss of $0.49 per share, and this is one data point. I am looking at one timestamp.

The test is whether or not it is able to convert its backlog into energized billable capacity without a credibility gap every time a powered shell is late to the party. It has proven it can take orders. Now it has to prove it can deliver the business model.

The NVIDIA (NVDA) announcement is a good story, but it also raises the margin of error. A $2 billion equity investment at $87.20 and a further development of a partnership to build AI factories is a validation of the business model, and it is. It is also a further step into the spotlight, where variability is punished most rapidly.

The Bull Case is Built on Power and Debt

The Q3 print gave me the right signal and the right reminder. Revenue, $1.4 billion, a 134% y/y growth, and a revenue backlog of $55.6 billion. The numbers are no longer theoretical. The company is announcing 41 data centers, 590MW of active power, and 2.9GW of contracted power. If I step back and look at the bigger picture, the 590MW operating and 2.9GW secured capacity define the bullish thesis.

CoreWeave is not just a cloud platform company; it is a company with a heavily financed balance sheet. They have generated an adjusted EBITDA of $838 million, with a healthy margin of 61% for the quarter. Looks good, doesn’t it? However, they still have an interest expense of $311 million and yet were able to report a GAAP net loss of $110 million. The operating engine is clearly powerful, but the cost of capital is still impacting their net profitability.

Adjusted EBITDA doesn’t flow to earnings. From EBITDA, they subtract depreciation and amortization related to accelerated GPU deployments, stock-based compensation, and approximately $311 million in quarterly interest expense. After that, their GAAP net loss is $110 million. This is largely a function of front-loading capital intensity, not demand weakness.

So, I’m going to get specific here. Last quarter, they told us that there had been a delay from a third-party data center provider. That delayed CapEx and capacity ramps into the next two quarters. In Q3, CapEx spend was around $1.9 billion. Construction in progress materially went up as the infrastructure waited to get deployed.

If Feb. 26 is another timing story, then the stock will rally. And it will rally the weak way, on story and supply as opposed to converting. And that isn’t really my favorite way to trade. But if they can show me that they are actually using that delayed capacity as promised and that revenue is simply being shifted to the right without any diminution of that value, then I can live with that. Delays? No problem. Delays that just keep happening? Problem.

What I’m Watching on Feb. 26

I’m not as focused on whether the top-line beat is $50 million and more focused on the cadence of execution. So, one thing I’m watching is more light on energized megawatts versus contracted capacity. They exited Q3 with ~590MW of capacity and expect more than 850MW of capacity active exiting 2025. If they reiterate these numbers without additional color, that’s a risk reducer for me.

The second thing I’m watching is the way the backlog is compounding. The backlog came in at ~$55.6 billion and had some nice color on diversification and large customers such as OpenAI and Meta. However, if the backlog continues to compound and the cadence of execution slows down, that’s not a good sign. That’s just inflation.

The last thing I’m watching is the capital position. Management’s guide to $12 to $14 billion of CapEx in 2025. At first blush, this doesn’t seem to be related to the $1.9 billion spent in Q3. The answer is on the bridge. The $1.9 billion spent in Q3 includes partial quarter energization, whereas construction in progress has materially increased with shells awaiting deployment.

A large amount of CapEx spending in 2025 has been weighted to backend GPU deliveries and large-scale cluster energization. Simply put, quarterly CapEx spending is lumpy, whereas annual CapEx spending includes infrastructure commitments already contracted but not yet revenue-generating.

However, that’s only true if financing continues to look favorably trending. If it starts to look more improvised than structured, I take a step back.

Valuation is a Timing Question

I understand CoreWeave’s valuation in terms of a discussion of time to earnings power, as opposed to a multiple discussion. The way the consensus works is that we’re going to grow from a $5.1 billion revenue run rate in 2025, grow to a $12.0 billion run rate in 2026, and then grow to a $20 billion run rate in 2027, with earnings per share becoming solidly positive at some point within the $20 billion range.

CRWV is trading at ~21x forward EV/EBITDA, which is lower than Nvidia’s ~32x. However, it’s slightly higher than NBIS’s ~18x. If we look at it on a trailing basis, it’s trading at 31x EV/EBITDA, again lower than Nvidia’s 39x. Forward EV/Sales is 13x, which is in line with IREN’s 14x. However, it’s lower than Nvidia’s 21x. If we look at it on a trailing basis, it’s trading at 8x Price/Sales, which is significantly lower than Nvidia’s 24x.

It’s an indication to me that the market doesn’t think of CRWV as a perfect execution story. There’s a certain level of capital intensity/risk involved in execution. If execution normalizes, multiple expansion toward higher-quality infrastructure peers is plausible.

Using a 20x EV/EBITDA multiple on a $7 billion 2027 EBITDA forecast, the enterprise value is $140 billion. Subtracting the $16.87 billion in net debt leaves the implied equity value at about $123 billion. Dividing this by ~368 million diluted shares outstanding, the implied fair value is about $335, which provides for a substantial potential upside if normal execution can be achieved.

CoreWeave's Biggest Risk

The most structural risk for CoreWeave is its customer concentration. While they’ve diversified their customer base, they still derive significant revenue and backlog from a small set of hyperscale AI labs and large tech platforms. These are massive, multi-year contracts, so they provide visibility, but they also provide lumpiness. If one of these large customers slows down, renegotiates, or decides to do things themselves, they will feel it.

The second structural risk involves Nvidia. However, it must be understood in the context of the AI Factory ecosystem. The approach by Nvidia is not to replace hyperscalers with neoclouds; rather, it is to increase total available capacity across both. CoreWeave acts as a high-density deployment specialist. The risk here is not Nvidia favoring hyperscalers, but it’s whether AI Factory deployments at hyperscalers vertically integrate faster than standalone capacity providers can differentiate on performance and speed to deploy.

Final Take

So, as of Feb. 26, I’m optimistic but not complacent. I would much rather add the stock as a function of the company delivering normalized demand than try to get in as the stock pops on the news.

Rather than waiting reactively for confirmation of the 850MW exit rate, I'm looking at leading indicators such as conversion rates of construction in progress, sequential growth of energized megawatts, conversion velocity of the backlogs to revenue, and any improvements in financing spreads.

If these indicators are improving ahead of the earnings announcement, it would be a great indication of normalization of execution ahead of the confirmation of the 850MW exit rate.

data.title: TDVG: Not A Bad Broad Market Option
data.description:
TDVG Overview

The T. Rowe Price Dividend Growth ETF (TDVG) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as an ETF) that invests in mostly large-cap, higher dividend-paying American-listed stocks. T. Rowe is pitching that they have the potential to outperform the broad market S&P 500 index using a disciplined portfolio construction process whereby they look at companies with both above-average earnings and dividend growth while looking to pick the winners. A team of industry-focused T. Rowe Price equity analysts is directly responsible for selecting stocks for the fund, as they attempt to beat the S&P 500 benchmark.

I have looked at other T. Rowe Price ETFs before and have seen some mixed results. For example, TSPA has had underperformance versus other low-fee competitors like SPY and VOO, though more recently it has performed about in line with its S&P 500 benchmark. That said, I am liking what I'm seeing about TDVG, whose benchmark target is also the S&P 500 (SPY); this article will tell you whether it's worth investing with T. Rowe's asset managers or whether you should just buy SPY, which is also an ETF but is passively managed with lower fees. That means its fund managers don't make many trades and only rebalance every once in a while, versus T. Rowe's managers, who have the mandate to buy and sell stocks more often if they feel that there's a compelling investment opportunity.

After further review, my analysis shows that TDVG is worth an investment. The fund is positioned well for a market broadening and has had a solid track record over the past couple years, with a lower beta to boot, as the fund is a bit more diversified across sectors.

2026 Outlook and Fund Breakdown

TDVG was launched on August 4, 2020, by T. Rowe with a mandate to create a fund that provided long-term capital growth with higher dividend payouts. T. Rowe noted in their prospectus to investors that the fund normally invests at least 80% of its net assets (plus any borrowings for investment purposes) in stocks that have a strong track record of paying dividends or are expected to pay dividends over time. At a glance, this doesn't really impress me, as paying out dividends is simply distributing excess cash that can't fuel growth. That said, when looking a bit deeper at the mandate, the prospectus notes that in choosing investments, the adviser looks for companies that it believes possess some of the following characteristics, including:

either a track record of, or the potential for, above-average earnings and dividend growth; a competitive current dividend yield; a sound balance sheet and solid cash flow to support future dividend increases; a sustainable competitive advantage and leading market position; attractive valuations, such as low price/earnings, price/cash flow, or price/sales ratios

This is where the magic will be made, as there are finally signs that the market has been broadening out in 2026, and institutional investors are now sounding the alarm that the market is expanding as AI and tech slip. Ultimately, T. Rowe is basically saying that they are launching a fund to track the S&P 500 and be more actively managed to generate additional alpha from those returns by diversifying across sectors and focusing on stocks with higher earnings - and over the past two years, the theme has quietly worked.

The fund employs a 0.5% yearly ETF expense ratio for the fees to the asset manager. Which is a little higher than I'd like, but the outperformance amidst an unprecedented bull run within high-growth sectors, I'm impressed.

Another interesting insight into the fund is how the managers have limited trading to outperform, with a portfolio turnover of just 14% in the recent fiscal year. This is low among active large-cap funds but signals that management is comfortable with its screening, which filters both high-dividend and high-earning companies, which are not always a category with large overlap. With broad diversification part of the mandate, a rate cut or two, which is what the market expects from new FED chair Kevin Warsh, should be a boon to this fund, which has cyclical sector exposure in financials, industrials, and healthcare in its top 4 sector holdings. Overall, the fund has done well and seems set up nicely for this next cycle of rate stabilization.

Previous Results & Fund Portfolio

When comparing TDVG to the S&P 500, the portfolio greatly resembles SPY in names, but there are variances among weights, with top holdings like AAPL seeing reduced weights and names like V, JPM, and GE getting some love. The fund has higher weightings in financials and industrials while cutting back on tech and consumer discretionary. I also believe their definitions for communications are a bit out of whack vs. SPY, but ultimately, it's a broad-based fund focusing on earnings growth and cutting out the rest of the noise. Over the long term, if the fund philosophy remains unchanged and if the 2026 theme of broadening continues, this fund could continue to outperform materially.

Risk Profile

From a risk perspective, the fund has a beta of 0.81. A beta of 0.8 notes that the fund has much less volatility than the benchmark - and that has borne out in past results. The fund has outperformed while keeping volatility in check - a nice plus for investors. Additionally, the Sharpe ratio measures risk-adjusted return, helping investors determine if they are being fairly compensated for the level of risk they are taking. The formula is the portfolio return minus the risk-free return, divided by the standard deviation. Essentially, it tracks the return required by investors for the risk they are taking, and it should be above 1 to indicate acceptable returns for the risk taken. The higher, the better. Given that 0.79 is above 0 but below 1, it indicates that the fund is generating some, but not much, excess return relative to its risk. Overall, though, it's a different way to play the US equity markets, and since inception, it's done a pretty good job.

Tom Huber manages the fund, and he has been in the business for 30+ years. Prior to assuming portfolio management responsibilities, he was a research analyst specializing in leisure, food and drug retail, and specialty retail. Before joining T. Rowe Price, he worked at Robert W. Baird as an assistant vice president managing IPO and merger and acquisition activity. It's usually a plus when I see sell-side analysts move to the buyside, as they often understand management and what makes a stock move. No red flags for me here.

Bottom Line

The bottom line for this fund, in my opinion, is that it's a solid option, with lower volatility and strong returns over the past two- and five-year windows. While the fee is a bit higher than I'd like, I think I can justify paying a 0.5% expense ratio compared to 0.09% for SPY or 0.03% for the Vanguard 500 Index Fund;ETF (VOO) and the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV).

This ETF has some differentiation, a lower beta, a large NAV over $1Bn, and sufficient liquidity. There are many alternatives to buy when trying to track and outperform the S&P 500 as an investor, but if you want a diversified fund with good market breadth, this is a solid choice.

data.title: Oakmark International Small Cap Strategy Q4 2025 Portfolio Drivers & Decisions
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Oakmark International Small Cap Strategy Q4 2025 Commentary.

The portfolio's return was 1.64% (NET) for the reporting period. This compares to the MSCI World ex USA Small Cap Index that returned 3.50% for the same period.

Top contributors:

  • Konecranes (KNCRY) was a contributor during the quarter. The Finland-headquartered industrial machinery company's stock price jumped as it reported earnings that exceeded market expectations. Comparable earnings before interest, tax, and amortization reached record highs driven by margin improvement in all three divisions. Order intake increased meaningfully in the equipment divisions, providing support for future growth. Later in the quarter, Konecranes increased its 2025 financial guidance and is now calling for higher profit vs. the prior year. We continue to believe that Konecranes' leading position in lifting equipment and related service position is supportive of long-term profitable growth.
  • Metso (OUKPY) was a contributor during the quarter. The Finland-headquartered industrial machinery company's stock price climbed as it delivered solid third-quarter 2025 results that were supportive of our investment thesis. Orders, revenue, and earnings before interest, tax, and amortization were all highlights—driven by particularly strong performance in the Minerals segment. We appreciate Metso's consistent execution and believe it is well-positioned to unlock further value.
  • Julius Baer (JBAXY) was a contributor during the quarter. The Switzerland-headquartered asset management and custody bank's stock price trended higher despite initially dipping after having posted largely positive results. The bank benefited from rising assets under management driven by strong market performance and well-managed cost control. The one negative was a higher-than-expected credit provision resulting from its credit review process, which began in February. We met with management and appreciate their focus on cultural change and performance improvement. Furthermore, we think they have done a strong job of mitigating credit issues, positioning the bank well for future success.

Top detractors:

  • TeamViewer (TMVWY) was a detractor during the quarter. The Germany-headquartered application software company's stock price slid after it reported underwhelming results and lowered its full-year annual recurring revenue ('ARR') guidance, citing general weakness and integration challenges with its acquirement of 1E, a digital employee experience firm. This shortfall in ARR this year also caused reduced expectations for reported revenue and earnings in 2026. While this news was disappointing, we believe many of these headwinds are likely temporary and that TeamViewer is trading at an extremely attractive valuation.
  • Azelis Group (AZLGF) was a detractor during the quarter. The Belgium-headquartered specialty chemical distributor's stock price declined as it reported soft results against a difficult comparison period. Earnings before interest, tax, and amortization were slightly weak, and the market environment continues to be challenging with decreased volume demand in Specialty categories and both volume and pricing pressure in the Commodity business. We met with management and remain confident they are taking the right tactical and strategic steps to unlock value in the future.
  • Wynn Macau (WYNMF) was a detractor during the quarter. The Hong Kong-based casino operator's stock price declined after earnings per share missed estimates, amid concerns about weak tourism and volatility in the Macau market. Still, revenues appeared solid, and we remain encouraged by the company's near-term plans to bolster its position in the Premium segment. In our view, Wynn remains well-positioned to unlock long-term value.

Portfolio Positioning

We initiated the following position(s) during the period:

  • Visional (VISIF) is a Japanese human resources technology company helping businesses hire and manage professionals more effectively. It is best known for BizReach, a rapidly growing three-sided platform connecting employers, headhunters and job seekers. BizReach is disrupting the traditional headhunter market by enabling efficient, high-quality direct hiring while benefiting from secular tailwinds stemming from increased job switching. Furthermore, we believe the company boasts an excellent management team that is making sound investments to create long-term shareholder value. Despite strong fundamentals, we believe the market is underestimating the scope of industry tailwinds and BizReach's leading position, creating an opportunity to invest in a business poised for long-term growth that is trading at a significant discount to our estimate of intrinsic value.

We eliminated the following position(s) during the period:

  • Gerresheimer (GRRMY)
  • JDE Peet's (JDEPY)
  • medmix (MDMXF)

Understanding the risks

All investments carry a certain degree of risk, including possible loss of principal. There is no assurance that an investment will provide positive performance over any time period.

Foreign securities present risks that in some ways may be greater than investments in U.S. investments. Those risks include: currency fluctuation; different regulation, accounting standards, trading practices and levels of available information; generally higher transaction costs; and political risks. Value stocks may fall out of favor with investors and underperform growth stocks during given periods. Smaller companies' stocks often involve more risk than the stocks of larger companies. Stocks of small companies tend to be more volatile and have a smaller public market than stocks of larger companies.
